content.text: Why Token Quota Forecasting Is an Architecture Concern

The Anthropic API enforces rate limits expressed in requests per minute (RPM) and tokens per minute (TPM). These limits are provisioned per API key and are shared across all integrations using that key. In an enterprise with multiple teams and integrations sharing a pooled API quota, token quota forecasting determines whether each integration can sustain its load, whether teams compete for the same resource, and when the organisation needs to request a tier upgrade.

Token quota forecasting belongs in the architecture process — not as a finance concern after deployment — because the quota required by an integration is determined by its design: the model tier, the context window size, the retrieval strategy, and the request volume. An integration that is over-designed (too many retrieved chunks, verbose system prompt) will consume two to five times the token budget of a well-designed alternative with the same quality outcomes.

An accurate token quota forecast is a consequence of a well-designed integration, not a number to be found after the fact. If the forecast requires more quota than the organisation can provision, the architecture must change.

attrs.alt: Token quota allocation hierarchy: organisation → teams → integrations with a 10% burst reserve

content.text: Understanding the Quota Dimensions
Requests Per Minute (RPM)

RPM limits the number of API calls per minute. Each call to the messages API endpoint consumes one request regardless of size. RPM becomes the binding constraint for integrations with many small, fast requests (e.g., a chat interface where each turn is a separate API call).
Tokens Per Minute (TPM)

TPM limits the total token throughput: input tokens plus output tokens across all concurrent calls in the minute. TPM is the dominant constraint for integrations that use large context windows — RAG integrations with extensive retrieved content, agent integrations that pass tool results, or summarisation integrations with long input documents.
Which Constraint Binds?
For a given integration, the binding constraint is whichever limit is reached first. Compute both and compare:
Token Consumption Forecasting
Bottom-Up Forecast from Integration Design

The most accurate token forecast is built bottom-up from the integration's design parameters. For each integration, the architecture team computes the token consumption from the request model and the context window design.
Top-Down Forecast from Historical Data

For integrations that have been running for at least 30 days, a top-down forecast from the audit log is more accurate than the bottom-up model. The historical data captures actual behaviour, including edge cases, long conversations, and peak usage patterns.
Allocation Models
Once the organisation's total quota is known, it must be allocated across teams and integrations. Three allocation models address different organisational structures and usage patterns.
Model 1: Static Allocation
Each team receives a fixed TPM allocation determined at provisioning time and reviewed quarterly. Allocations do not adjust dynamically based on actual usage.
When to use:
Stable workloads with predictable usage; organisations that require cost predictability above all else; teams with strict isolation requirements.
Headroom requirement:
Each team's allocation should exceed its P99 TPM by at least 20%. Teams that consistently hit 80% of their allocation trigger a reallocation review.
Limitation:
Unused capacity cannot be redistributed in real time. If Team A is under-utilising its allocation during a period when Team B is near its limit, Team B still rate-limits.
Model 2: Proportional Allocation with Rebalancing

Each team receives a proportional share based on its measured consumption over the prior period. A monthly rebalancing process adjusts allocations to match actual usage patterns with a cap on single-period changes.
Model 3: Burst Reserve Pool

A portion of the organisation's quota (typically 10–15%) is withheld from static allocations and held in a shared burst pool. Integrations that need temporary additional capacity can borrow from the pool, subject to priority rules and time limits.
Pool size:
10–15% of org TPM. Larger pools provide more elasticity but leave less for static allocations.
Borrowing rules:

A team may borrow from the pool for up to 4 consecutive hours per day. Borrowing requires an active incident or a planned event (product launch, peak season) documented in the change management system.
Priority:
Customer-facing integrations have priority over internal tooling when competing for burst pool capacity.
Replenishment:
The pool replenishes automatically each hour. It cannot be pre-emptively reserved — it is claimed on demand.
Monthly Quota Review Process

Token quota allocations must be reviewed monthly to remain accurate. Usage patterns change as integrations mature, user adoption grows, and new integrations are deployed. An allocation that was correct at launch may be 50% too small or 100% too large six months later.
Review Action
Trigger
Owner
Outcome
Reallocation (minor)
Any integration consistently above 80% of its allocation for 2+ weeks
Platform team
Increase allocation from reserve pool or rebalance across teams
Reallocation (major)
Any team consistently above 80% of its allocation for 2+ weeks
Architecture lead
Proportional rebalance; may require tier upgrade request
Tier upgrade request
Org total P95 TPM > 70% of provisioned quota
Engineering director
Submit upgrade request to Anthropic; provision additional quota
Downsizing
Any integration P95 TPM < 30% of allocation for 4+ weeks
Platform team
Reduce allocation; reallocate freed capacity to reserve or other teams
Integration retirement
Integration decommissioned
Integration owner
Remove allocation; return to reserve pool
Growth Forecasting

Quota allocations must account not only for current usage but for anticipated growth. The three growth drivers for Claude integrations are user adoption curves, use-case expansion (more conversation turns, longer documents), and new integrations onboarding.
Growth Rate Models

# quota/growth_model.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
import math

@dataclass
class GrowthModel:
    current_monthly_tokens: int
    model_type: str  # "linear" | "exponential" | "s_curve"
    growth_rate_pct_per_month: float = 0.0  # for linear
    doubling_months: float = 0.0            # for exponential
    saturation_multiple: float = 0.0        # for s-curve (ultimate / current)

    def project_months(self, months: int) -> list[int]:
        projections = []
        for m in range(1, months + 1):
            if self.model_type == "linear":
                projected = int(self.current_monthly_tokens * (1 + self.growth_rate_pct_per_month / 100 * m))
            elif self.model_type == "exponential":
                k = math.log(2) / self.doubling_months
                projected = int(self.current_monthly_tokens * math.exp(k * m))
            elif self.model_type == "s_curve":
                # Logistic growth
                saturation = self.current_monthly_tokens * self.saturation_multiple
                k = 0.3  # growth steepness
                x0 = 6.0  # inflection at month 6
                projected = int(saturation / (1 + math.exp(-k * (m - x0))))
            else:
                projected = self.current_monthly_tokens
            projections.append(projected)
        return projections

# New integration: early adoption phase → exponential until plateau
model = GrowthModel(
    current_monthly_tokens=50_000_000,
    model_type="s_curve",
    saturation_multiple=8.0,   # expect 8× current at full adoption
)
projections = model.project_months(12)
for m, tokens in enumerate(projections, start=1):
    print(f"Month {m:2d}: {tokens/1_000_000:6.1f}M tokens/month")
Quota Runway Calculation

The quota runway is the number of months until the integration's P95 TPM is projected to exceed its allocation. When runway drops below 3 months, the team must initiate a reallocation or tier upgrade request.

# quota/runway.py
def compute_quota_runway(
    projections: list[int],
    current_allocation_tpm: int,
    business_days_per_month: int = 22,
    peak_hour_factor: float = 4.0,  # peak minute is 4× monthly avg rate
) -> int:
    # Convert monthly tokens to peak TPM
    for month, monthly_tokens in enumerate(projections, start=1):
        avg_tokens_per_minute = monthly_tokens / (business_days_per_month * 8 * 60)
        peak_tpm = int(avg_tokens_per_minute * peak_hour_factor)
        if peak_tpm > current_allocation_tpm:
            return month - 1  # months of runway remaining before breach
    return len(projections)  # projection window not breached
Quota Monitoring and Alerting
Quota monitoring is a continuous operational requirement. The three alert tiers for quota headroom:
Alert Level
Condition
Action
Owner
Warning
P95 TPM > 70% of allocation for 3 consecutive hours
Review request volume; check for unusual traffic; document in weekly ops report
Operations engineer
Critical
P95 TPM > 85% of allocation for 1 hour
Request burst pool loan; reduce retrieval chunk count if RAG integration; page integration lead
On-call engineer
Emergency
Any minute exceeds allocation (rate limit responses observed)
Immediately activate degraded mode (fewer chunks, shorter history); page integration lead and platform team
On-call engineer (P1 incident)
Summary

Token quota forecasting is a design-time activity, not a post-deployment measurement. The bottom-up forecast derives the required TPM from the integration's context window design and usage model; the top-down forecast refines this estimate with historical data once the integration is running. Three allocation models — static, proportional with rebalancing, and burst reserve — address different organisational needs. Monthly quota reviews and continuous monitoring with tiered alerts ensure that allocations remain accurate as usage grows and new integrations are deployed.

The quota runway calculation makes growth visible: when projected usage is within 3 months of the current allocation, the team must act. Proactive tier upgrade requests — submitted before the allocation is breached, not after — are the difference between a controlled capacity expansion and a production rate-limit incident.
